section.title: The grand translation table
section.p: opened the proof idea: prove the theorem on simple pieces, then let interior boundaries cancel. That proof idea explains why one formula can wear so many familiar faces: \int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_M d\omega.
Now we slow down and make the translation explicit.

subsection.title: A small dictionary before the table
subsection.p: The same symbols appear again and again.
A scalar function is a 0-form: f. Its exterior derivative is the 1-form df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy+f_z\,dz.
So the old vector calculus operations appear as:

table.title: Old vector calculus operations as exterior derivatives.

subsection.p: This dictionary is the key to the translation table.

subsection.title: Dimension 1: intervals, functions, and endpoints
subsection.p: Let M=[a,b], oriented from a to b. Its boundary is \partial M=\{b\}-\{a\}. The form on the boundary must have degree 0, so it is a function: \omega=f. Then d\omega=df=f'(x)\,dx.

Stokes' theorem becomes \int_{\partial[a,b]} f=\int_{[a,b]}df. The left side is \int_{\partial[a,b]} f=f(b)-f(a), and the right side is \int_{[a,b]}df=\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx. Therefore \boxed{f(b)-f(a)=\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx.}
This is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
The interval is the first place where the boundary carries signs. Without the sign \{b\}-\{a\}, the theorem would not know the difference between f(b)-f(a)\qquad\text{and}\qquad f(a)+f(b).

subsection.title: Dimension 2: regions, 1-forms, and boundary curves
subsection.p: Let M=R be an oriented plane region with positive orientation dx\wedge dy. Its boundary \partial R is oriented so that the region stays on the left.
The form on the boundary must have degree 1. Write \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy. Then \boxed{d\omega=(Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy.}
Stokes' theorem becomes \int_{\partial R}P\,dx+Q\,dy=\int_R (Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy. In ordinary vector calculus notation, \boxed{\oint_{\partial R}P\,dx+Q\,dy=\iint_R (Q_x-P_y)\,dA.}
This is Green's theorem in circulation form.

There is also a flux version. For a plane vector field \mathbf F=(A,B), outward flux across the positively oriented boundary is represented by the 1-form \phi=A\,dy-B\,dx. Then d\phi=(A_x+B_y)\,dx\wedge dy. So Stokes gives \boxed{\oint_{\partial R} A\,dy-B\,dx=\iint_R (A_x+B_y)\,dA.} In vector notation, \boxed{\oint_{\partial R}\mathbf F\cdot\mathbf n_{\mathrm{out}}\,ds=\iint_R \nabla\cdot\mathbf F\,dA.}
Thus Green's theorem has two common dialects:
The theorem is the same. The 1-form decides what the boundary is measuring.

subsection.title: Surfaces in space: 1-forms and curl
subsection.p: Now let M=S be an oriented surface in space. Its boundary \partial S, if it has one, is an oriented curve. The boundary orientation is determined by the chosen side of the surface.
The form on the boundary is again a 1-form: \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz. This is the work form of the vector field \mathbf F=(P,Q,R).
Stokes' theorem becomes \int_{\partial S}\omega=\int_S d\omega. In vector notation, \boxed{\oint_{\partial S}\mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r=\iint_S(\nabla\times\mathbf F)\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS.}
This is classical Stokes' theorem.

table.title: Forms language and vector language for surfaces.

subsection.p: Green's theorem was the flat version of this story. Classical Stokes lets the inside surface bend.

subsection.title: Solids in space: 2-forms and divergence
subsection.p: Finally let M=E be an oriented solid region in space. Its boundary \partial E is an outward-oriented surface.

The form on the boundary must have degree 2. For a vector field \mathbf F=(P,Q,R), use its flux 2-form: \boxed{\Phi_{\mathbf F}=P\,dy\wedge dz+Q\,dz\wedge dx+R\,dx\wedge dy.} Then \boxed{d\Phi_{\mathbf F}=(P_x+Q_y+R_z)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz.} The coefficient is \nabla\cdot\mathbf F.

Stokes' theorem becomes \int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\mathbf F}=\int_E d\Phi_{\mathbf F}. In vector notation, \boxed{\iint_{\partial E}\mathbf F\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}_{\mathrm{out}}\,dS=\iiint_E\nabla\cdot\mathbf F\,dV.}
This is the divergence theorem.

table.title: Forms language and vector language for solids.

subsection.p: Here the boundary is not a curve. It is a surface. That is why the boundary form has degree 2, not degree 1.

subsection.title: The same theorem in four familiar disguises
subsection.p: All four translations can be placed in one table.

table.title: The same theorem in four familiar disguises.

subsection.p: A useful way to read the theorem is:

p.md: \boxed{\text{boundary accumulation of a form}=\text{interior accumulation of its derivative}.}

subsection.p: In one dimension, the boundary is two signed endpoints. In two dimensions, the boundary is a signed curve. In three dimensions, the boundary of a solid is a signed surface. The word signed is doing quiet work in every case.

subsection.title: A translation checklist
subsection.p: When a problem mentions one of the classical theorems, translate it by asking four questions.

row.cell: What is the inside object M?
What is its oriented boundary \partial M?
What form \omega is being integrated on the boundary?

subsection.p: For example, if a problem asks for \oint_C \mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r and C bounds a surface S, the boundary form is \omega_{\mathbf F}=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz. Then the interior form is d\omega_{\mathbf F}=\Phi_{\nabla\times\mathbf F}. So the problem is a Stokes problem.

If a problem asks for outward flux through a closed surface, the boundary form is \Phi_{\mathbf F}. Then the interior form is d\Phi_{\mathbf F}=(\nabla\cdot\mathbf F)\,dV. So the problem is a divergence theorem problem.

This is not just a memory device. It is a way to avoid using the wrong theorem. A line integral of a 1-form belongs on a curve. A flux 2-form belongs on a surface. A volume 3-form belongs inside a solid. The degree tells us where the form can live.

The table makes the theorem look tidy. But tidy tables can hide danger. The formula works only when its grammar is respected: the orientation must be induced, the whole boundary must be present, the form must be defined where the theorem uses it, and the smoothness assumptions must be honest. The next section is a collection of failures. They are not exceptions to Stokes' theorem. They are examples of what happens when we try to speak the theorem's sentence with one word missing.
